Launch Partnership
Three Main Types of Deals

TRANSCRIPT
In this third video on how to manage other people's launches, I want to talk about
deals and the three main types of deals. The first thing I just want to mention is
that in business, every deal is a deal. I know that sounds a little crazy, but these are words
of wisdom I've lived by for a long time. Every deal is a deal, meaning that I'm going go
over some of the basics of how to negotiate. In fact, the next video is going to be all about
how to negotiate and what to negotiate. The thing I want to have in the back of your mind
right now is that all this is malleable. There is no contract that was carried down, carved
in stone that said, "This is how you help people with their launches, and this is what you
get paid, and this is how you get paid." Everything is negotiable. The reality is, like I said
in the last video, that the most important thing is getting the right client.
It's like in baseball, and I hate to use a baseball analogy because I know it's not
played all over the world, but you can stand at the plate there, and they throw balls and
you try to hit the ball, right? This game is like you're the batter, and they're throwing balls
to you, but you can wait as long as you want. You don't have to take the next pitch. You
don't have to take the next deal. It's about getting the right client and negotiating the right
deal. Just know that once you get into negotiations, anything's possible. You can define it
any way you want. You can define this business any way you want. You can create the
business that you want. Every deal is a deal, and everything is negotiable.
I'm going to go over the three primary ways of doing this. What I want you to know
is that I've done all three of these, and I've done all three of these more than once. This is
all from hard fought experience; I made a lot of money. Some of the deals have gone
really well, other deals, they've gone okay, and some deals fell apart. I've got a lot of
experience to share with you.
The first one is the simplest, and this is just where you're either
consulting or you're coaching. You're not actually producing. You're not doing the
work, but you're just either meeting in person or getting on the phone for a defined
amount of money.
One time I did this for Bill Glazer and Dan Kennedy. They had a product they were
about to roll out, and they just really wanted to get my eyes on it. My brother and I flew
out, spent the entire day with them, they paid us $10,000. This was way back. You can't
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

Launch Partnership Bonus – Three Main Types of Deals
3
© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc. 	I 	www.ProductLaunchFormula.com
get me for $10,000 for a day now. Not to brag, I'm just saying don't call me up looking for
that deal. In fact, I'm basically un-hirable at this point just because of the realities of my
business has grown so much that I can't afford to take days and work on other people's
businesses. They paid me to come out, spend a day together, and we talked about their
launch, and what the offer would look like, and what the Prelaunch would look like, and
how they would rally partners. This type of deal is very, very simple. It's just x number of
dollars for x amount of time. It could be a one shot deal. That was a one shot deal, one
day, we were in and out, super clean. I didn't have to worry about anything.
Or it could be a monthly deal, or it could be a weekly. It could be a series of calls.
Just because I got paid $10,000 for a day doesn't mean you have to. You could do a day
for a lot less, or you could do it for a lot more, or it could be an hour, or it could be a half a
day, it could be a Skype session. It could be four calls. It could be anything you want at
any dollar amount you want. The reality is, at the end of the day, you're just coaching
them. You're saying, "Did you think about this? Did you think about that? You might want
to do this. You might want to do that. That plan looks too aggressive. The timing on this
looks a little crazy. Yeah, that script looks fantastic. You might want to add in these bullet
points. I think the offer's a little weak here. You might be able to get these partners there."
Anything like that, it's just coaching. It's consulting. It's very simple. This is by far the
simplest deal to do.
The next type of deal to do is, and again, this is one I've done multiple times,
and this is where you actually partner with someone. This is where someone,
typically they might have a domain of knowledge. They're the expert. They're the talent,
so to speak. Then you're providing the marketing. This is actually how I got started in this
business. I had someone who had been a mentor for me in the financial markets. We
decided to start publishing in the financial markets about how to trade the financial
markets. We did a 50/50 deal. The way it worked is he provided the content, and I did
everything else in the business, which was customer service, all the tech, all the actual
publishing, all the marketing, all the website building, all the systems, all the accounting,
everything that an online business takes, which is a lot. In the end, the content piece,
which seemed like a really big deal at the beginning, that ends up being a pretty small
piece. In this case, the 50/50, at least in my mind, worked out really well for my partner
because he was just spending half hour a day to write up the content each day, and I was
spending 8 or 10 hours a day to build the business, but that's the deal we did.
An interesting thing here is that after about eight years in the business, my partner
basically stole the business. He took the business and he created a competing website.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

4
© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc. 	I 	www.ProductLaunchFormula.com
After watching me do all the work for eight years, he thought, "Oh, Jeff, he's getting half
the money. It's not really worth it. Plus, I know all of his tricks by now, so I can do my
own marketing." I tell you that just so that you know that that's not the first time this type
of deal has broken down that way where the content producer really started to think that
they were the star, they're the star of the show. It's my show. I can take it and go do what I
want with it. That has happened to more than just myself. I know of several people it's
happened to. That is a tendency for the talent, so to speak, to think that they are the
business. It's something to watch out for.
The reality is this deal actually better works as 70/30, 70% for the marketing and
the business and 30% for the talent. If you look at what talent's paid in other areas, say
like in Broadway, or Hollywood, or in the athletics, I don't know the numbers but I don't
really think the talent tends to get 50%. I think that talent tends to get less than 50%. The
reality is the talent is replaceable. The business is not replaceable. The marketing is not
replaceable. The talent is. If you look at someone like, just to show how old I am, Johnny
Carson used to do late night TV. I don't watch enough TV, I can never remember what his
show was called, but it was a late night talk show. He was on for 30 years. I went to
Wikipedia, it said from '62 to '92. He was this icon, and he seemed irreplaceable. He was
this amazing talent. Then he retired, and they replaced him with Jay Leno. It was no
problem. Jay just went on, just no problem. Then Jay was replaced by Conan O'Brien. The
talent is replaceable.
When you negotiate this deal, if you're bringing real marketing experience, if you're
bringing real capability here of your business building experience, consider that 50/50 is
not the way you should do that deal. It should be 70/30. That might be hard for the
"talent" to hear, but that's the reality - that pretty much in any type of show business and
in any type of business where there's a talent, talent is replaceable. The business is not
replaceable. I just put that out there. I have done deals multiple times 50/50. Many of
them worked out really well. That one worked out well for eight years and then it went a
little sideways. This is a way to build a real business where you have real skin in the game.
The third model is what we call the PLM model, the Product Launch
Manager model. I'm going to be going deep on this in another video, but I just want to
mention. This is like we're actually running the launch for someone. When you're doing
this, just in terms of the deal, first of all, picking the right horse is super incredibly
important here because you'll be putting a lot of work in. Like I said, one time I did it, and
I put a lot of work in, and the product never launched. The person never brought me a
finished product, could never get it done. The reality is I've got projects I've never

© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc. 	I 	www.ProductLaunchFormula.com
completed. We all probably have projects we haven't completed. You have to make sure
you're working with someone that's going to complete the product. You have to get the
right horse.
Then this is absolutely critical. You have to get paid up front. The way I would
typically structure this deal is I would get paid x number of dollars up front. That's just to
sign the deal, to get on my calendar, to get on my schedule. Back in the day, this is a
decade ago, I was typically paid $10,000 to $15,000 just to get on the calendar, and then I
would get paid a percentage for the launch. The way we structured it was we looked at
what a good launch would be, and it was 10% of the gross for the launch. That was pre-
refunds. It was 10% of the gross of the launch, and then if we exceeded what was a good
launch, it gradually went up to 15%. Maybe it was the first million dollars was 10%, and
then after a million dollars it was 11%, and after $1.2 million it was 12%, and after $1.5 it
was 13%, and after $2 million it was 15%. You want to negotiate whether those bumps are
retroactive for the whole amount or just stair stepped.
Remember, every deal's a deal. It's all negotiable, but you have to define this stuff.
Part of doing this deal is having it be crystal clear at both sides on what they're paying for,
what they're going to get, what you're going to do, how the numbers are going to get
calculated. It doesn't have to be some fancy legalese. I'm a fan of actually having
something that's very readable, having a one-pager that's super readable, that regular
humans, you don't need lawyers to be able to understand what it is. You have to do this.
You have to get the upfront money because at the end of the day, you're going to
put a lot of time and effort into this, and you have to carve out a place in your schedule.
You can say, "August and September, I'm going to work on this launch." If you're doing
that, if you're going to sign this deal and you're going to block off your calendar, you need
to compensated for that. That money is not refundable. If they don't end up ever
launching the product, you keep the money. That deal I made where it never shipped,
where we never actually launched, I didn't get any upfront money on that one. I screwed
up. This is how I learned this lesson. You need some upfront money.
The other thing you need is a retainer. Say they want to launch in September or
October. That means you're going to be working in July and August. Maybe you're signing
the deal in June. You sign the deal in June, they give you x number of dollars, and then
for each month, they have to pay you x number of dollars. Back then for me, upfront was
$10,000, but then each month it was $500. $10,000 up front plus $500 a month. Then if
the launch gets delayed an extra three months, fine, but they keep on paying you that
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc. 	I 	www.ProductLaunchFormula.com
$500. This is basically your compensation for having this big project in your head and in
the offing that you have to have on your calendar, that you've blocked off that chunk of
time on your calendar. Upfront, plus retainer, plus percentage.
Every deal's a deal. You could make that upfront higher or lower, and the
percentage higher or lower, and the retainer higher. All of those amounts are going to be
negotiable. If you're just brand new to this and you haven't ever done one, don't be
thinking that Jeff got $10,000, so I have to charge $10,000. If $5,000 makes sense to
you, do it that way. If $1,000 makes sense for you, do it that way. You have to work with
someone that's willing to put skin in the game and pay you up front because you have skin
in the game. You have blocked off this area on your calendar. You're going to be putting in
a lot of work before that launch. It's going to be a couple months of work, and then that
launch happens. That's probably not going to be another 30 days until you get paid. It's
going to be a long time with you not getting paid, so you need to be paid up front. You
need to be paid that retainer. Do not be flexible on these at all. Oh, by the way, when
you're doing that deal, negotiate in exactly when you get paid. How many days after the
launch does that percentage come in?
Those are the three main ways to do this. One is very simple, the least lucrative but
super simple is the coaching/consulting. The second is partnering, and that's a big deal.
It's a long-term commitment. I did that for my first eight years in this business. It can be
great. It can be fantastic, but there are a few things to watch out for. Then there's that
PLM model. One final thing about the PLM, the Product Launch Manager model, one
final piece about that is that there are a lot of people looking for launch managers. There
are more opportunities to be a launch manager than there are launch managers out there.
Really, you just have to do one good launch, and after you do one good launch, then
you're going to get more clients through word of mouth than you can imagine.
In saying that, it's not like this is the greatest, it's free money. You're going to have
to work at it, and you're going to have to focus on client selection. You're going to have to
pick the right horse. If you do that and you have one success, then you're going to have
more clients than you could shake a stick at, than you can imagine, than you can handle,
put it that way. You can't do 30 of these a year. You just can't because it does take over
your life for a few months, really. Yeah, finding clients is not going to be your problem.
Finding the good clients and picking the good clients is going to be the issue.
In my next video, I'm going to talk a little bit about the actual negotiations and
doing the deal.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com
